study.journal: ICU-ROX Investigators and the Australian and New Zealand Intensive Care Society Clinical Trials Group. Conservative Oxygen Therapy during Mechanical Ventilation in the ICU. N Engl J Med. 2020.

clinical_question.condition: Patients undergoing mechanical ventilation in ICU

population_context.population_description: 1000 adult patients aged ≥18 years who were anticipated to require mechanical ventilation in the ICU beyond the day after recruitment.
population_context.exclusions: Not fully reported in provided text; see Supplementary Appendix

intervention.name: Conservative oxygen therapy
components: Upper alarm limit for SpO2 set to 97%
FiO2 decreased to 0.21 if SpO2 above acceptable lower limit
Supplemental oxygen discontinued in extubated patients if SpO2 above acceptable lower limit
Minimize exposure to SpO2 of 97% or higher
intervention.parameter_values: SpO2 target range: lower limit 90%, upper limit alarm 97%; FiO2 reduced to 0.21 or decreased by at least 0.05 at intervals of 30 minutes or less
intervention.timing: From randomization until ICU discharge or day 28, whichever was earlier

comparator.description: Usual oxygen therapy with no specific measures limiting FiO2 or SpO2; use of upper alarm limits for SpO2 was prohibited; use of FiO2 less than 0.3 during invasive ventilation was discouraged

outcome_effect.outcome_name: Number of ventilator-free days from randomization until day 28

strengths: Large multicenter randomized controlled trial with 1000 enrolled patients
Prospective design with predetermined protocol registered before enrollment completion
Independent data and safety monitoring committee oversight
Clear randomization procedure with computer-generated random numbers and stratification by center
Multiple secondary outcomes including mortality at 90 and 180 days, cognitive function, and quality of life
limitations: No significant difference in primary outcome between groups (median VFD 21.3 vs 22.1 days, p=0.80)
Trial group assignment known to clinical staff but not to patients/families (single-blinding)
Enrollment restricted to patients with <2 hours of mechanical ventilation, limiting generalizability to earlier ICU course
Mortality at 180 days showed trend toward increased mortality in conservative oxygen group (35.7% vs 34.5%, OR 1.05, 95% CI 0.81-1.37) though not statistically significant

applicability.generalizability_notes: Findings apply to adult ICU patients anticipated to require mechanical ventilation beyond the day after recruitment. Results may not generalize to patients with <2 hours of baseline ventilation at enrollment or to specific subpopulations such as ARDS or severe hypoxemia requiring high FiO2.

quoted_sentences.design: ICU-ROX was an investigator-initiated, parallel-group, randomized clinical trial.
quoted_sentences.intervention: In the conservative-oxygen group, the FiO2 was decreased to 0.21 and supplemental oxygen was discontinued in patients who had been extubated if the SpO2 was above the acceptable lower limit. In this group, we sought to minimize exposure to an SpO2 of 97% or higher by mandating the use of an alarm that was set to sound when the SpO2 was 97% whenever supplemental oxygen was administered in the ICU.
quoted_sentences.results: The number of ventilator-free days did not differ significantly between the conservative-oxygen group and the usual-oxygen group, with a median duration of 21.3 days (interquartile range, 0 to 26.3) and 22.1 days (interquartile range, 0 to 26.2), respectively, for an absolute difference of −0.3 days (95% confidence interval [CI], −2.1 to 1.6; P = 0.80).
quoted_sentences.outcome: In adults undergoing mechanical ventilation in the ICU, the use of conservative oxygen therapy, as compared with usual oxygen therapy, did not significantly affect the number of ventilator-free days.
